codeSnapshot.label: convolution.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torchvision.transforms as T
from PIL import Image
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# --- 1. Single Conv2d layer ---
conv = nn.Conv2d(
    in_channels=1,   # grayscale input
    out_channels=8,  # 8 filters => 8 feature maps
    kernel_size=3,   # 3x3 filters
    stride=1,
    padding=1        # same padding: output H,W = input H,W
)
print(f"Filter shape: {conv.weight.shape}")  # [8, 1, 3, 3]
print(f"Bias shape:   {conv.bias.shape}")    # [8]

# --- 2. Forward pass ---
x = torch.randn(1, 1, 28, 28)  # batch=1, channels=1, H=28, W=28
out = conv(x)
print(f"Input:  {x.shape}")    # [1, 1, 28, 28]
print(f"Output: {out.shape}")  # [1, 8, 28, 28] (same padding)

# --- 3. Edge detection with a hand-crafted Sobel filter ---
sobel_x = torch.tensor(
    [[-1, 0, 1], [-2, 0, 2], [-1, 0, 1]], dtype=torch.float32
).reshape(1, 1, 3, 3)

img = torch.randn(1, 1, 64, 64)  # dummy grayscale image
edges = nn.functional.conv2d(img, sobel_x, padding=1)
print(f"Edge map shape: {edges.shape}")  # [1, 1, 64, 64]

eli3: Imagine sliding a small magnifying glass (the filter/kernel) across a photo. At each position, you multiply what you see under the glass by the numbers in the filter and add them up. That sum is one pixel in the output image. Slide the glass all the way across and you get a whole new image called a feature map.

Different filters detect different things: one filter might light up wherever it sees a vertical edge, another where it sees a diagonal line, another where there's a certain colour. During training, the network learns which filters are useful for the task — you don't design them by hand.

The key advantage over a regular fully-connected layer is parameter sharing: the same filter is used at every position in the image. A 3×3 filter has 9 weights and that's all — whether the image is 32×32 or 1000×1000. This makes CNNs much more efficient for images and gives them translation equivariance.

whenWhy.title: Image recognition and computer vision tasks
whenWhy.body: Convolutional layers are the backbone of every vision model: image classification (ResNet, EfficientNet), object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab). The convolution operation exploits two properties of images: locality (nearby pixels are more correlated than distant ones, so a 3×3 filter captures the relevant context) and translation equivariance (the same feature — an edge, a curve — should be detected regardless of where it appears in the image). These inductive biases make CNNs far more sample-efficient on image data than fully-connected layers.

whenWhy.title: 1D convolutions for sequence data
whenWhy.body: Conv1d applies a kernel along the time/sequence dimension: useful for audio waveforms, time series, and NLP (TextCNN). A 1D convolution with kernel size k and stride 1 extracts local patterns of length k — analogous to n-gram detection in text. Compared to RNNs, 1D convolutions are parallelisable (no sequential dependency) and have fixed receptive fields, making them faster but less capable of capturing very long-range dependencies without stacking many layers.

whenWhy.title: Feature extraction and transfer learning
whenWhy.body: Convolutional feature maps from pretrained networks (ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet trained on ImageNet) are powerful general-purpose visual features. Freezing the convolutional layers and training only the final classification head (feature extraction) requires minimal data and compute. Fine-tuning the entire network on a target dataset with a lower learning rate for early layers is the standard approach for domain adaptation. This works because early convolutional layers learn universal features (edges, textures) that transfer across domains.

deepDiveIntro: The 2D convolution operation in deep learning slides a small filter (kernel) across the input image. At each position, it computes a weighted sum of the input patch under the kernel. For a layer with multiple input channels and multiple output channels, there is one kernel per output channel, and each kernel covers all input channels. The total parameter count is the number of output channels times the number of input channels times the kernel height times the kernel width — independent of the spatial dimensions of the input. Compared to a fully connected layer, this is a massive reduction in parameters. [1]

Two inductive biases make convolutions well-suited for images: locality (each output depends only on a small neighbourhood of the input) and weight sharing (the same kernel is used at every spatial location). Weight sharing means a feature detector learned at one position applies everywhere — implementing translation equivariance. Note that pooling converts equivariance into invariance at coarser scales. [2]

deepDive.heading: Output Dimension Formula
deepDive.body: The output height and width depend on input size, kernel size, stride, and padding. With same padding (P=1, stride=1, kernel=3), the output has the same spatial dimensions as the input. With stride=2, the output is roughly half the spatial size — this is how strided convolutions replace max pooling for downsampling. The total parameter count for one conv layer is: (output channels) × (input channels × kernel height × kernel width + 1 for bias) — independent of the input spatial dimensions. [1]

deepDive.heading: Receptive Field and Depth
deepDive.body: The receptive field of an output unit is the region of the original input that affects it. A single 3×3 conv layer has a 3×3 receptive field. After L such layers, the receptive field grows roughly linearly with depth. Strided convolutions or pooling cause the receptive field to grow faster — each stride-2 step doubles coverage in input pixels. Dilated (atrous) convolutions expand the receptive field without adding parameters by skipping positions in the kernel — used in semantic segmentation models like DeepLab to see large context without losing resolution. [2]

deepDive.heading: Depthwise Separable Convolutions
deepDive.body: Standard convolutions mix information across both channels and spatial positions simultaneously. Depthwise separable convolutions split this into two steps: first apply one spatial filter per input channel (no cross-channel mixing), then apply 1×1 convolutions to mix channels. This gives roughly a 9× reduction in computation and parameters for a 3×3 kernel compared to standard convolution. This is the foundation of MobileNet, EfficientNet, and Xception — architectures designed for efficient inference. [2]

deepDive.heading: Visualising What Filters Learn
deepDive.body: Zeiler & Fergus (2014) used deconvolution networks to visualise what each convolutional filter has learned. Layer 1 filters detect oriented edges, colour blobs, and Gabor-like patterns — similar to what is found in the mammalian primary visual cortex. Layer 2 combines these into textures and simple shapes. Layer 3 detects higher-level structures (wheels, faces, text). This hierarchical feature learning is why pretrained CNN features transfer to new domains: low-level features (edges, textures) are domain-general. [1]

takeaways: 2D convolution (cross-correlation): (I★K)[i,j] = Σ_{m,n} I[i+m,j+n]·K[m,n]; parameter sharing across spatial positions. [1]
Output size: H_out = floor((H + 2P − K)/S) + 1; parameter count: C_out×(C_in×K²+1), independent of input size. [1]
Local connectivity and weight sharing give CNNs translation equivariance and sample efficiency on images. [1]
Receptive field grows as O(K·L) with depth L; dilated convolutions expand it without adding parameters. [2]
Depthwise separable convolutions reduce computation ~K² times vs standard convolutions (foundation of MobileNet). [2]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between convolution and cross-correlation, and does it matter in practice?
edgeCases.a: True convolution flips the kernel before sliding: (I * K)[i,j] = Σ_{m,n} I[i−m,j−n]·K[m,n]. Cross-correlation does not flip: (I ★ K)[i,j] = Σ_{m,n} I[i+m,j+n]·K[m,n]. PyTorch and all major frameworks implement cross-correlation and call it 'convolution'. This does not matter in practice because the kernel weights are learned — if the network needs the flipped version, it can learn a flipped kernel. The distinction matters only when computing convolutions by hand with hand-designed kernels (e.g., Sobel filters) or when working with signal processing conventions.

edgeCases.q: Why are 1×1 convolutions useful, and when are they used?
edgeCases.a: A 1×1 convolution with C_out output channels is a learnable linear combination of input channels at each spatial position — equivalent to a fully-connected layer applied independently at every spatial location. Uses: (1) Channel dimensionality reduction (bottleneck layers in ResNet, Inception): reduce C_in=256 channels to C_out=64 before an expensive 3×3 conv, reducing compute by 4×; (2) Channel mixing after depthwise convolutions (MobileNet pointwise step); (3) Increasing model depth without increasing receptive field. The key property: 1×1 convs mix information across channels while preserving spatial structure, whereas 3×3 convs mix information across both channels and spatial neighbourhoods.

sources.label: LeCun et al. (1998) — Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition, Proceedings of the IEEE 86(11)
Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (2016) — Deep Learning, MIT Press (Chapter 9: Convolutional Networks)
